questions.text: The theory that life originated from pre-existing non-living organic molecules is:
questions.explanation: Oparin and Haldane proposed that the first form of life could have come from pre-existing non-living organic molecules.

questions.text: S.L. Miller created primitive earth conditions in a laboratory. What were the gases used?
questions.explanation: Miller used a closed flask containing these gases at 800°C and electric discharge.

questions.text: Which of the following was NOT found in Miller's experiment?
questions.explanation: Miller observed the formation of simple organic compounds but not complex polymers like DNA.

questions.text: The wings of a bird and the wings of a butterfly are ________ organs.
questions.explanation: They have different anatomical structures but perform the same function (flying).

questions.text: The presence of homologous organs indicates:
questions.explanation: Homology is based on common ancestry and shows how the same structure evolved in different directions.

questions.text: Analogous organs are a result of:
questions.explanation: Unrelated organisms evolve similar traits to adapt to similar environments.

questions.text: Which of the following is a vestigial organ in humans?
questions.explanation: Vestigial organs are remnants of structures that were functional in ancestors.

questions.text: The industrial melanism observed in the peppered moth (Biston betularia) is an example of:
questions.explanation: Dark moths had a survival advantage in soot-covered industrial areas, showing selection in action.

questions.text: Darwin's finches in Galapagos islands show:
questions.explanation: Different species evolved from a single ancestral seed-eating finch to occupy different niches.

questions.text: Lamarck's theory of evolution was based on:
questions.explanation: Lamarck believed that use and disuse of organs led to changes inherited by offspring.

questions.text: Who proposed the 'Mutation Theory'?
questions.explanation: Hugo de Vries worked on evening primrose and suggested that large mutations cause evolution.

questions.text: According to Darwin, the main cause of evolution is:
questions.explanation: Darwin emphasized small, continuous, and directional variations.

questions.text: The Hardy-Weinberg principle states that allele frequencies in a population remain constant in the absence of:
questions.explanation: Genetic equilibrium is maintained if there is no evolutionary force acting on the population.

questions.text: In the equation p² + 2pq + q² = 1, the term 2pq represents the frequency of:
questions.explanation: p and q are allele frequencies; p² and q² are homozygous frequencies; 2pq is the carrier frequency.

questions.text: When a small group of individuals migrates to a new location and establishes a new population, it is called:
questions.explanation: The genetic makeup of the new population reflects the small sample of the founders.

questions.text: Which type of natural selection favors both extreme phenotypes and acts against the mean?
questions.explanation: Disruptive selection leads to two peaks in the phenotypic distribution.

questions.text: Stabilizing selection results in:
questions.explanation: It favors the intermediate phenotype and reduces variation.

questions.text: The chronological order of human evolution is:
options: Ramapithecus -> Australopithecus -> Homo habilis -> Homo erectus
Australopithecus -> Ramapithecus -> Homo erectus -> Homo habilis
Homo habilis -> Homo erectus -> Australopithecus -> Ramapithecus
questions.explanation: This follows the fossil record from ape-like ancestors to modern humans.

questions.text: Which human ancestor was the first to use fire?
questions.explanation: Homo erectus (Java man/Peking man) is associated with the first use of fire and stone tools.

questions.text: The brain capacity of Neanderthal man was approximately:
questions.explanation: Neanderthals had a large brain size, similar to or even slightly larger than modern humans.

questions.text: The first human-like hominid was:
questions.explanation: Homo habilis is often called the 'handy man' and is considered the first of the genus Homo.

questions.text: The term 'Saltation' used by Hugo de Vries refers to:
questions.explanation: He believed evolution was a jerky process driven by large mutations.

questions.text: Fitness, according to Darwin, refers ultimately to:
questions.explanation: Organisms that leave more progeny are considered more fit in an evolutionary sense.

questions.text: The concept of 'Omnis cellula-e cellula' was given by:
questions.explanation: It means all cells arise from pre-existing cells, supporting the idea that life doesn't arise spontaneously.

questions.text: The Big Bang theory explains the origin of:
questions.explanation: It describes a singular huge explosion that led to the expansion of the universe about 20 billion years ago.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a pre-requisite for Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires a large population to minimize the effects of genetic drift.

questions.text: Marsupial radiation in Australia is an example of:
questions.explanation: A variety of marsupials evolved from a common ancestor within the isolated continent.

questions.text: Flippers of Penguins and Dolphins are:
questions.explanation: They are similar in function (swimming) but have different anatomical origins.

questions.text: Louis Pasteur disproved spontaneous generation by using:
questions.explanation: He showed that life did not appear in sterilized broth unless exposed to dust/microbes from the air.

questions.text: The first non-cellular forms of life could have originated ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: The first non-cellular forms like RNA/proteins are thought to have appeared 3 billion years ago.

questions.text: Evolution is a ________ process.
questions.explanation: Evolution is based on chance events in nature and chance mutations in organisms.

questions.text: Herbaceous lycopods and arborescent lycopods evolved from:
questions.explanation: Zosterophyllum was a common ancestor for early vascular plants.

questions.text: The 'Age of Reptiles' was the:
questions.explanation: Dinosaurs and other reptiles dominated the Mesozoic era.

questions.text: Coelacanth is considered a:
questions.explanation: It was thought to be extinct but was found alive, representing a lineage of lobefinned fish.

questions.text: Human settled in caves and started cave art about:
questions.explanation: Pre-historic cave art developed around 18,000 years ago during the ice age.

questions.text: Which of the following is the 'Missing Link' between reptiles and birds?
questions.explanation: Archaeopteryx had both reptilian (teeth, tail) and avian (feathers, wings) characteristics.

questions.text: Biogenetic Law (Ontogeny recapitulates Phylogeny) was proposed by:
questions.explanation: He suggested that the development of an embryo repeats the evolutionary stages of its ancestors.

questions.text: Which factor increases variations in a population?
questions.explanation: Mutations introduce new alleles and increase the genetic diversity of a population.

questions.text: Darwin traveled in which ship?
questions.explanation: He went on a 5-year voyage around the world on the HMS Beagle.

questions.text: Agriculture and human settlements started about:
questions.explanation: Neolithic revolution (agriculture) began roughly 10,000 years ago.
